name: NewsTilt
summary: A YC-backed platform intended to help journalists build online businesses; the founders closed it after an eight-month company life and two months in market.

factors.evidence: The co-founder described major communication problems, divergent motivation, and an inability to continue working together as the most important company-level factors.
Journalists stopped contributing and the product never attracted a large reader audience after launch.
The company lacked enough money to repair the product problems and the founder believed raising more would have been difficult.

name: Homejoy
summary: An on-demand home-cleaning marketplace that ceased operations after struggling to raise its next round amid worker-classification litigation and weak service economics.

sources.title: Homejoy Is Shutting Down At The End Of The Month

factors.evidence: Reporting at closure said Homejoy had been unable to raise sufficient capital for a Series C after raising nearly $40 million.
Worker-classification lawsuits and a related California labor ruling reportedly made prospective investors wary of the category.
Postmortem reporting described individual cleaning transactions as unprofitable and customer retention as difficult.

name: Quibi
summary: A mobile-first short-form streaming service that announced a wind-down roughly six months after launch and sought to sell its content and technology assets.

sources.title: High-profile streamer Quibi is shutting down after subscriber struggles

factors.evidence: The company said its standalone streaming idea may not have been strong enough and acknowledged that the service was not succeeding.
The company identified its pandemic launch timing as a likely contributor while noting that other businesses adapted successfully.
Contemporary reporting said the service failed to attract enough viewers willing to pay despite $1.75 billion in financing.

name: Cydoc
summary: A bootstrapped health-AI company that built automated clinical intake tools and closed after seven years when low revenue, integration costs, and a difficult sales model made the business unsustainable.

sources.title: Why I Shut Down My Bootstrapped Health AI Startup After 7 Years: A Founder’s Postmortem

factors.evidence: The founder calculated roughly $30 of revenue above hosting cost per doctor while each required EHR integration cost thousands of dollars, creating an untenable payback period.
The founder identified inadequate sales and marketing as her single biggest mistake, leading to too few customers and insufficient revenue.
Not integrating with incumbent EHR systems made adoption too difficult and contributed directly to customer loss.
The solo founder lacked complementary ownership of sales and marketing and described leadership capacity as insufficiently distributed.

name: SimpleDirect Financing
summary: A contractor-financing product that was sunset after its high-touch service costs converged with or exceeded per-customer revenue; the company subsequently pivoted to open-weight AI models.

factors.evidence: The company reported annual revenue of about $1,500 per contractor against service costs of roughly $1,300 to $1,900, calling the mismatch structural.
Rather than close the legal entity, the team sunset the financing product and redirected the company toward open-weight AI models.
Customers wanted high-touch, phone-based support while the product was priced and designed for self-service.

name: Fieldbook
summary: A hybrid spreadsheet-and-database product that struggled to reconcile a complex relational model with self-service growth; the team accepted a talent acquisition and shut down the product.

factors.evidence: The founder concluded that relational data modeling was too complex for Fieldbook's low-price, self-service growth model and made onboarding unusually difficult.
The company remained understaffed, struggled to recruit engineering and design talent, and fell behind on activation and performance work.
Fieldbook remained below $60,000 ARR in early 2018 and could not see a path to grow fast enough to reach profitability.
The founders chose a talent acquisition as the best available path despite expecting it to end the product.

name: Hivebeat
summary: A student-event platform that pivoted into organization-management SaaS and closed after failing to establish a focused, differentiated product and predictable growth model.

sources.title: Why we’re shutting down Hivebeat and what we’ve learned along the way

factors.evidence: The founder said Hivebeat never reached real product-market fit because its product was too generic for a niche-oriented market.
The product was not sufficiently differentiated and lacked category-specific features available from competitors.
The team attempted too many product and marketing initiatives simultaneously and accumulated technical debt through an unfocused MVP.
A transaction-based model produced unpredictable revenue and a volatile growth curve.

name: Unifyo
summary: A relationship-management startup that moved through six products before reaching an enterprise sales use case, but lacked the focus, bandwidth, and sales expertise to pursue it successfully.

factors.evidence: The founder said the team lacked the bandwidth and enterprise-sales experience required to succeed with its final B2B proposition.
Unifyo built six products through six pivots, reflecting an extended search for a viable customer and value proposition.
The company validated the enterprise buyer and sales motion too late after initially targeting end users and small businesses.

name: IntroNet
summary: A professional-introduction platform that closed its applications after failing to establish sustainable product-market fit before its cash ran out.

sources.title: Mike Krupit’s startup IntroNet shut down. Here’s what he learned from its demise

factors.evidence: The founder said IntroNet failed to find sustainable product-market fit before reaching the end of its cash.
Weak activation and insufficient network data made the company uninteresting to outside investors as cash depleted.
The founder later cited insufficient hands-on leadership and a distributed team as constraints on execution and agility.

name: Flud
summary: A social newsreader that raised seed capital and attracted early attention but suffered product-quality and adoption problems, pivoted late toward enterprise, and ran out of runway before securing another round.

sources.title: Why Startups Fail: A Postmortem For Flud, The Social Newsreader

factors.evidence: Flud failed to close an additional financing round and exhausted its runway before the enterprise product had enough operating history for investors.
The founder said the initial product was insufficiently tested; launch traffic exposed bugs, delays, and scaling failures that damaged early adoption.
The consumer product struggled with monetization and plateauing adoption, while the later enterprise opportunity arrived too late.
The company initially declined enterprise demand and returned to it only after consumer fundraising became difficult, leaving too little time to validate the pivot.

name: Spreadable
summary: A Grasshopper Group referral-marketing product that was still growing and generating revenue when the company stopped investing, kept it dormant through 2011, and planned a year-end closure to refocus resources on larger opportunities.

sources.title: Why We Shut Down a Business That Was Making Money & Getting New Customers Every Day

factors.evidence: The company concluded that the addressable market for its word-of-mouth marketing tool was significantly smaller than the markets for its other products.
Management believed the same financial and team resources would produce a better return if invested in Grasshopper and reassigned most of the six-person core team.
The postmortem said acquisition cost and customer payback were too high to scale despite ongoing signups and revenue.
A core rendering change created customer-specific CSS failures, cancellations, and unsustainable support work before the team reverted the architecture.

name: Torrenegra Organization
summary: A bootstrapped Latin American founder program that reached profitability and $20,000 to $35,000 in monthly sales, but voluntarily ended all programs after the founders concluded the regional market could not support the scale or impact they sought.

factors.evidence: The founders did not believe the company could scale tenfold in Latin America and concluded that the market's natural ceiling was below their ambition.
Even with millions of annual organic content impressions, attracting enough additional companies remained difficult across repeated experiments.
The profitable programs were closed because their expected impact and financial return did not justify diverting the founders from larger opportunities.

name: Sip by Product Hunt
summary: A Product Hunt mobile and web technology-news digest that reached more than 100,000 monthly web readers but was discontinued after weak retention, absent product-led distribution, and growth that cannibalized the parent platform.

factors.evidence: Web expansion increased readership tenfold, but web users were much less sticky and the growth did not produce sustainable adoption or retention.
Sip depended on Product Hunt's newsletter, homepage, and social channels for traffic, reducing attention and engagement elsewhere in the core business.
The team underestimated the daily effort required to build and manage Sip and failed to set deadlines that would force prioritization and tradeoffs.

name: Flynn
summary: An open-source, self-hosted platform-as-a-service that operated for years with a skeleton team and service revenue, then shut down after failing to raise a Series A or fund continued product development alongside round-the-clock customer support.

factors.evidence: The cofounder said Flynn could not attract long-term project funding or raise additional venture capital after its seed round, including a Series A.
Operations-as-a-service revenue could not cover both ongoing product and feature development and the 24/7 on-call team required for paying customers.
The round-the-clock support burden became too much for the skeleton team, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic.

name: Evopass
summary: A ticketing startup founded by three Trinity College Dublin students that closed its platform after partnerships failed to materialize, new entrants arrived, and the market leader added face-value resale, eroding its differentiation.

factors.evidence: New entrants appeared and the incumbent market leader introduced face-value resale, eroding Evopass's remaining unique value proposition.
After six months of work, planned partnerships had not materialized and the team concluded the business was no longer sustainable despite some ticket sales.
The founder said first-time-founder optimism led the team to overweight positive signals and adopt data-driven decision-making too late.
